title: Gallic War VI.13-18: The Druids and Celtic Religion
description: Caesar's account of the Druids: their doctrine of the immortality of the soul, their authority, and their human sacrifices.
introduction: Caesar's ethnographic excursus in Book VI of the Gallic War is the most detailed classical account of Celtic religion. He describes the Druids as judges, teachers, and theologians who taught the transmigration of souls — a doctrine that Greek observers compared to Pythagoreanism. Despite Caesar's political motivations, this passage remains indispensable for understanding pre-Christian Celtic belief.

translation.original_text: In omni Gallia eorum hominum qui aliquo sunt numero atque honore genera sunt duo... Druides... Equites.
translation.text: In all of Gaul there are two classes of men of any account and dignity... the Druids and the Knights.

translation.original_text: In primis hoc volunt persuadere, non interire animas, sed ab aliis post mortem transire ad alios, atque hoc maxime ad virtutem excitari putant metu mortis neglecto.
translation.text: Above all they wish to persuade people that souls do not perish but pass from one body to another after death, and they think this is the greatest incentive to courage, since the fear of death is thereby set aside.
